text: Management of a severe stricture after sleeve gastrectomy, conversion to gastric bypass.
The patient is a thirty nine year old female.
Her past medical history is significant for gastroesophageal reflux disease and anemia.
The past surgical history is significant for laparoscopic sleeve gastrectomy in
The patient complains of severe reflux and insufficient weight loss.
The patient underwent an EGD and an upper GI, where a narrowing was identified at the level of the gastric pouch, resulting in a proximal dilatation of the stomach.
The patient decided to undergo a robotic-assisted conversion from sleeve gastrectomy to Roux-en-Y gastric bypass, resection of the stricture.
The trocars are positioned, with the camera port placed to the left of the umbilicus, indicating the setup for the robotic-assisted procedure.
Two robotic ports are visibly placed in the patient's left flank and left upper quadrant, facilitating access for the robotic-assisted surgical procedure.
A robotic trocar is placed in the right upper quadrant to facilitate the positioning of the robot's right arm.
The assistant trocar is positioned between the camera arm and the right robotic port, completing the setup for the robotic-assisted procedure.
The surgeon begins the procedure by lysing adhesions between the previous sleeve gastrectomy and the liver, carefully removing all adhesions from the sleeve using robotic instruments.
The surgeon identifies a dilatation in the proximal part of the sleeve gastrectomy, visible through the camera view.
The surgeon creates a window posterior to the gastric sleeve and transects proximal to the stricture, forming a pouch.
The camera view reveals the stricture within the sleeve gastrectomy site, highlighting the narrowed segment of the gastric sleeve.
The surgeon uses a stapler to transect the sleeve distal to the stricture, resulting in the resection of the stricture at the level of the pylorus.
The surgeon retracts the transverse colon to visually identify the Treitz's angle, an anatomical landmark in the abdominal cavity.
The surgeon transects the jejunum loop approximately thirty centimeters distal to the Treitz's angle, using surgical instruments to separate the intestinal segment.
The surgeon measures and runs the alimentary limb approximately 120 centimeters, performing a side-to-side jejuno-jejunostomy at this level using a surgical stapler.
The surgeon advances the alimentary limb cranially and performs a gastrojejunostomy, connecting the stomach to the jejunum.
The surgeon begins the gastro-digenostomy by placing a running posterior suture using 3-0 PDS suture material.
After completing the outer-posterior layer, the surgeon opens the gastric pouch, revealing the interior of the stomach.
The surgeon creates an enterotomy in the jejunum using a monopolar hook, visibly opening the intestinal wall.
The surgeon performs an inner-posterior layer suture using 3-0 PDS, completing the gastro-digenostomy with precise stitching visible in the surgical field.
The surgeon begins suturing the anterior aspect of the anastomosis using a running 3-0 PDS suture, completing the connection between the gastric pouch and the jejunum.
The surgeon begins a running suture from the opposite side of the anastomosis, joining it with the previously placed suture in the middle, and ties them together to enhance the reconstruction.
The surgeon completes the inner and anterior layer of sutures, then performs a second layer on top using a running suture technique.
The operating time was two hundred and two minutes.
The estimated blood loss was fifty centiliters.
No complications were registered.
A postoperative upper GI showed no leaks and the anastomosis was permeable.
